So, there's really only two ways. We spread the word, we spread the gospel, we spread the word, we spread the legend. We either do it through manual labor, our own or others, or we do it through media. There's really only two ways we can do it. And the good news is now we have a lot of media. When I compare it to my earliest days in all of this 40 years ago, you had fingers left over when you named your media options. You not got more than that in Amazon. If you just cut up all the things you can do in Amazon, you got more media alternatives that we had total, total. And that's very good news and it's very bad news. It's very good news is because, man, the toolbox is nearly infinite. The really bad news is a lot of people feel compelled to use every tool in the toolbox. They democratize them. They don't rank them. They don't focus on one better than the other, one better for their market than the other. And they tend to start to think in terms of new media and old media. And you hear this conversation all the time. If you read the marketing trade journals, Target, DM News, Ad Age, those kind of things, this is the conversation. So are you working with new media or are you working with old media? Well, it's a specious conversation. What we really want to talk about is effective media, ineffective media, profitable media, unprofitable media, media that serves our purposes, media that doesn't serve our purposes. Who cares if it's old or new? What difference does that make? Does that have anything to do with anything? Ultimately, when you and I go make a bank deposit, which is what all this is ultimately about, we do not get a bonus for using the newest media. The teller doesn't go, oh, you've mastered Facebook. I'll add 20% to your deposit today. The teller doesn't go, shit, you made this money with Valpek? I'm deducting 20% from your deposit because you're using old media. Money doesn't work that way. Money doesn't care. It has no preference whatsoever about any of this. So you can't either. So in some respects, we're blessed and at the same time, we're cursed. And it becomes very important to develop a sane and rational and manageable media strategy for what it is that you are setting out to do and who it is that you are setting out to become. So my wealth manager client who only wants people who sold a company for $10 to $50 million, which nearly guarantees, and especially the rest of his story, nearly guarantees they're going to come to him between the ages of 57 and 72. And they're going to have a certain value perspective. There's a lot we know about them. And one of the things we definitely know about them is they're not using Facebook. Now, they have a staff person who's using it, maybe, but they aren't. And we are not going to effectively reach them that way. And they do not want to communicate with us that way. So it's not a matter, is that a good media, a bad media? Is it new or old? Is it something I like or something I don't like? None of that matters. It's all about effectiveness, right? So media strategies. First of all, which is, I would say it regardless of where I was. So this is not pandering to Nick and Jack and stuff, but you either publish or you perish when you set out to do this stuff. Being published matters, and being published a lot matters a lot. If you can sum up my whole author published strategy, you can sub it up as better to be prolific than good. It's not a single thing I've ever done that couldn't be done better. That happens for two reasons. One, because it gets a side topic, but everything gets a money budget. So I know what every hour has to be worth and every day has to be worth because at their minimum I can make 19 grand a day as many days as I care to work with people coming to me. If I'm going to do a book, the next No BS book, the brand building by direct response book that we just did, I can figure out by now there's a history. I pretty much know what the direct monetary gain from that book is going to be. That gets divided into the number of hours, number of days it can have. It's done when it has used up its days. If we're on chapter 14, guess what? This book got 14 chapters because we're done. Peyton don't get a fifth quarter. Game over. So I have game over. Not secondly, it's because it's more important to have a lot of stuff out there and to keep having stuff going out there than it is to have the best thing out there. I'm not getting paid by literary critique. I'm not and nobody in this room cares about that. Nobody sat and went through the story you told me about No BS marketing, the affluent book and you raised all your stuff and the staff mutiny but when it's all said and done you had your best month, the story you told me. You didn't sit there and look for typos. You didn't do a grammatical critique, a literary critique. You don't really care. That's not what our relationship author to reader is about. Now if I'm writing to school teachers, different story. But for most of us, we are in the a lot beats best. So there are people who work, well even less. So Les turns out about one to two fiction books a year. Most years one and he works six to seven hours a day for 11 months to do a book. Non-fiction is much easier than fiction. I do three days. Three days. Gone. Next. Because I like need to put a dozen things out there. I need not one. So you need to publish because there's two things in America where you make yourself irrelevant. If you aren't published and if you aren't seen on TV. So if you do both of those, you can get away with one or the other maybe but you damn sure can't get away with both. Leno will discover to his shot in surprise six months from now that he is not really all that welcome in Vegas every week on Friday night doing a live show and didn't pay a lot of money for it. Because it's going to be Jehu real quick. Just as it's Oprah who. Real quick. And she's still on but she's on channel 16,233. I mean, I don't know how to break it to her on TV. So the most important thing is there's platforms. Legends have their own typically their own platform and they access other people's platform as well. And there's three things to understand about other people's platform. There's other people's customers, which is often the most valuable thing that there is. There's other people's resources and there's other people's money. That's other people's platform. So let's talk about how we access other people's platforms. I'll just hit on a few. Some that people don't think about. So number one, products. Information products or entertainment products. How to products. Products. Books, manuals, CDs, DVDs, courses, online courses, productizing who you are and what you do and getting it distributed in distribution channels. That's a media strategy. It is not about getting rich from it necessarily. It is about having ways for people to find you who you want to find you who might otherwise not find you. So this wealth advisor client of mine who has spent a quarter of a million with me, give or take. Here's where he came from. My article in Success Magazine. He shouldn't even be reading Success Magazine. He's ten levels above it but he reads everything. Any business success, personal development, he's like reading it. So he's reading the airy fairy, metaphysical, sitting the woods magazine and he's reading Forbes the same night. So that's the best of my knowledge. That's the only thing that came out of it. One guy. But what do you found me if I wasn't there? I don't know. Arguably not. He didn't find me before. So there's no certainty he was going to find me afterwards. Nightingale Conant. So a lot of product. But far more importantly are the lifers on the GKIC side and the clients on the client side who came because they saw magnetic marketing advertised in a Nightingale Conant. There's clients that have been worth over a million dollars to me who bought magnetic marketing from Nightingale Conant, not from the Nightingale Conant catalog because Nightingale Conant also had distribution in Sky Mall. The catalog and airplanes were sharper image and all those other guys are. And I think we were woefully unsuccessful in Sky Mall except this guy bought a magnetic marketing from Nightingale Conant then found his way to me and then spent a million dollars. Now I could keep, I could do hours of these antidotes. And so product distribution. Providing who you are, your story, your content, your methodology so that it can be distributed into more distribution channels the better that's a media strategy. Providing free content is a media strategy more people are far more aware of and cognizant of and do but often don't do on as organized a basis as they possibly could. So for example because I work with a lot of information marketers I have a few products that are designed, were specifically designed for them to use has stick gifts. A stick gift is something that goes when somebody buys a product so they don't return it for a refund. And so it's designed to sort of be intimidatory. So we have two, we have a book called The Ultimate Success Secret which people buy the rights to and co-author. And it was built to be a stick letter. That's really what it is. And the message of the book is you not get results is not the fault of the thing you bought or the person you bought it from it's because you aren't taking any action dipshit. And they can't really say that to them to their customer but they can have me say that to their customer. The other one which we now are close to 300,000 copies of this thing in circulation. It's a little booklet called Why Do I Have to Sit Next to the Farting Cat? And people buy them in bulk to throw in shipments as stick gifts. Every one of them things leads back to El Dano. And they haven't brought 300,000 people. But in bunny they'd be pretty profitable. So free content. Availability is a guest expert. Guest ways to do that, informal ways to do that. But there's a lot of lazy folks out there who have a lot of media who need free content and they need guest experts. A conversation amongst Nick and Jack and they're in my titanium group this year is like I'm running out of people to interview who fit the following criteria. Saying not stupid, actually know something, won't do something insane if they get a, who do you know who I should interview? Barter. Nothing wrong with barter. I've got some people over here you might want access to. You got some people I might want access to. You got one kind of media. I got a different kind of media. Outright quid pro quo. Mentorship. You help somebody instead of quid pro quo media but they have media. Business outright, right of check. We talked about that. Being a third party brokering relationships. This is very, very useful. So I'll tell you the best story about this very quickly. So business consultant, his name is Summers White. He's almost retired now. Just had dinner with him in Phoenix. So Summers came up as a speaker but he's really a fairly high priced management consultant mostly in finance comes from the banking industry. So his feeder system for years was his peers in the national speakers association because he only needed a dozen good clients a year and if he had enough speakers talking about him he became sought after. So his strategy was one of creating obligation that ethical people felt a need to discharge by third party brokering of relationships. So here's Summers entire years marketing activity. It's one dinner. The night before the NSA convention a black tie fancy dinner. Which guys all hate but the wives all loved it. Because how often, how often am I calling Carl and up saying get the gown out. Not often. So women dressed to the nines, guys stuffed into tuxedos. After the first couple years they wouldn't let him do it in the same hotel as the convention anymore so he had to do it offsite. So you have everybody arriving but you have maybe out of a thousand you have maybe 200 who are dressed up like penguins and prom queens and are getting in limos and taxis and going somewhere and then three, four hours later they're all coming back. And everybody's hanging out in the bar and of course now wants to know what's it like, where'd you go, what's it like. So he would do these dinners by invitation only. If you went pretty soon everybody was cornering you, can you get me in, how can you get me in, how do I get in, how can you. And before and in between courses Summers would go around, first of all he seated you strategically. So God knows how much time he spent on the seating chart but to put you next to in a cross phone somebody that you might have something legitimate to talk about. And then he would go around with a microphone and he would get almost everybody up. Sometimes not everybody, first time or sometimes not but he would get everybody up and he would have which he prepared ahead of time a little three or five card, three questions to ask you about your area of expertise that he was pretty sure you could answer and make yourself look smart. Now nobody did and here's my website and here's how, it wasn't that but you got to do demonstration. That's what you got to do. And then people sought you out and everybody sought each other out. And by the way halfway through the dinner the guys all had to turn a plate over, there was a number, all the men had to move, the women stayed put and so he really seated everybody twice during the course of the night. So this thing took forever, forever, right. But you got business out of this and you got good business out of it, not everybody but a bunch of us. I mean I went twice and it turned into money every single time really without me breaking a sweat trying to make it happen. Now all these people owe him, how do they discharge this obligation if they have any integrity at all? It's not your barber or your newspaper kid, you can't tip them at Christmas. So some people send a gift but you got a really wealthy guy here who don't eat gifts, what do you got to gift them? But what you can do is tell somebody about him. This is his home marketing, filled his whole book of business every year and didn't do anything else, every year. Put himself in the center, was a third party broker of relationships, created obligation. That's a media strategy. Upstream. So this is largely, so here specifically is what has fed me a lot. So I have helped a whole lot of people become niche information marketers. That's not what I am, I'm Butcher Baker candlestick maker, that's not what Nick is, and Jack, Butcher Baker candlestick maker. But the niche people are like a guy who builds a really successful dental practice with great marketing or is the number one Invisalign person or whatever and now turns around in one way or another packages that up and sells that back into that niche. So dentist to dentist, restaurant owner to restaurant owners, plumber to plumbers, heating an air conditioning guy to heating an air conditioning guy, financial advisor to financial advisor, etc, etc, etc. I've spawned thousands of them, hundreds of them successful with million to multi million dollar businesses. Their average core herd is three to five thousand of whatever they are. So three to five thousand plumbers, three to five thousand real estate agents, three to five thousand this, three to five thousand that. So here's what they are effectively doing. Craig Proctor every year in real estate spends a million dollars sifting through that giant haystack of real estate agents, most of whom are worthless to me and rounding up the smart, ambitious, progressive ones who are really investing in and working on making their businesses better and then he tells them about me. And a significant number of them say, gee, I'm hearing about this Kennedy guy from Proctor. I also heard about him from this guy. I also heard about him from this guy. I also heard about him from this guy. The smart one says, I think I'll swim upstream. I got the best one there was and I didn't have to deal with all the others. That's an upstream media strategy, which gets me to one page over the feeder system. Key word system. You don't want to be fed by random accident. You don't want to be spent. You don't want to be fed by desperate prospecting. You want to be fed by a system. You want your own media platform. You want others media platforms. You want your legend being spread. And ultimately you want funnels. You want people being sent to you, told about you, and when they come to find you, you have an organized marketing and sales funnel that they fall into. And then there's one thing for them to do and another thing for them to do and another thing for them to do. So this is why a lot of people in my businesses, speaking, consulting, authorship, why even people like the Kardashians run the risk of winding up broke. Because to use internet language, they create massive amounts of traffic, but they have no organized system for monetizing it. So all sorts of people come to see them. However it is, they see them. They go look at their videos on YouTube. They come to their website. They download free this, free that. They get the daily blog. They come to a nightclub and look at them. They come to a show and see them. So think about entertainers. If you go, so entertainers caught on, gee, we should sell stuff at the back of the room like Zigg. So a lot of times there's t-shirts. There's CDs of the act. There's no capture. They don't know who bought. In many cases it was cash in a box. And everybody goes home and they have no means of turning that into a business. So very famous speaker, name I will not name. It could actually be any number of people. Roughly same amount of time in the business as me. 35 years. At least 10 times as many people through his fingers as through mine. He's at least 10 times more famous than I am. And he needs the money from tomorrow's gig to pay last week's bills after 35 years. I do not. Very simple difference. He just let people see him and let people leave. No funnel, no capture, no continuous or sequential monetization. So fame in and of itself tends to do this. And dump you back down the other side of it. Part of your feeder system should be what I call omnipresence. So omnipresence is about within a target audience, they can't turn in a circle 360 degrees at any time in 24 hours and seven days, 365 days out of the year without seeing you or hearing you or being reminded of you. You got to figure out who that's right to do and how to do it. So that's why I want a lot of books out there, not just one or two. Because I want them on four different bookshelves in your office. So you bump into them here and there. I want Dan Kennedy bobblehead on the desk. I want Dan Kennedy mouse pad, I want Dan Kennedy t-shirt. So when you go in your closet and try and pick what you're going to wear to go work on a car on Saturday, you see me again. I want two different newsletter packages a month, not one. I want a weekly memo. I want omnipresence. So I'm a Disney guy. You cannot be in my house if you have any observatory powers whatsoever or downstairs in my office for more than five minutes and not know I'm a Disney guy because there's Disney crap everywhere. It's inescapable. There's a Disney talking clock. There's giant Disney watch. There's Disney figurines. There's Disney posters, Disney calendars, Disney stuffed animals, Disney clothes, Disney every, Disney, Disney, Disney, Disney. They've permeated the place. I can't, it's impossible to go a day and not think of Disney because I can't, I can't turn around. I can't even use it downstairs bathroom without encountering Disney. Every time I go pee, I think of Disney. Omnipresence. Some things to know about media, as you put it in your feeder system. Print is mostly about credibility, authority, and longevity. Broadcast is about celebrity and visibility. Products and distribution gives it all to you. It's the only media that does. There's events, spectacles, and stunts. And online media is predominantly for viral purposes. You must be very careful about making too much free and viral. And about defensive purposes. I have to do it because everybody else is doing it. Which by the way, years ago used to be the yellow pages. So different media serves different purposes. If you want to see media strategies, I gave you a couple of lists. So we won't spend time on them. 54 and 55, I gave you Jesus Christ and Christianity. And all the different media being used. Under manual labor, by the way, they call it evangelism. Werner Erhard at S called it sell it by zealot. It's exactly the same thing, by the way. A mobilization of an unpaid sales force with a script. That's what it is. Print, obviously. Broadcast, obviously. By the way, the first TV ministry was not evangelicals at all. It was Catholic. Bishop Sheen, black and white, somebody to Google. Spectacles, certainly Christianity's got them. Stunts. And then online media. If you look at the superhero list, you will see. Same media used much the same way. You want, this is another list to make for you. This is your media use list. Here's every media. How am I using it now? How could I use it that I'm not using it now? So it's a fifth implementation list. If you quickly look just some examples from me. If you look at 56. So Amazon, bunzenobled.com, bookstores. That has always been a big part of my media platform. The best customers for me come from there. The best clients come from there. Amazon, incidentally, a lot of people don't know it. They're not a store. They're a search engine. But they're the only search engine used only by people trying to buy something. Media advertising for the books. So all things being equal, there's 15 different small publishers I could be at. I chose Entrepreneur for a simple reason. I get two full page ads in Entrepreneur magazine for every book. Articles, we syndicate articles for free, for everybody to use. Piracy and theft, a big part of my platform. So eBay is the largest criminal conspiracy in the world. If it were a bunch of retail pawn shops, they'd all be in jail. Because a huge amount of their revenue comes from the sale of stolen merchandise and stolen intellectual property. And we spent about a decade trying to stop it. And then much like Amazon and online taxes, we finally said we can't stop it. So all we can do is make sure we embed real well in everything they're going to steal and sell, pathways that bring people to us. And so a lot of people will apologetically come up to me and sort of sheepishly, I bought your thing on eBay. And that's what led me. And so they're now at a super conference. They're paying $2,500 to be at. They're buying $3,000 stuff while they're there. And what brought them to me is that they bought a product on eBay that essentially was stolen from me that I didn't get any money on. I'd like 1,000 of them a day. Go ye forth and copyright infringed. Because here it comes, right? Paid exposure. So I try and get paid to also be exposed. This is paid and exposure. For me, it's a whale hunting exercise. Maybe there's one person in the room for whom I can do a big project. But they'll also be feed to GKIC that wasn't before and so forth. So I try and get paid for Cardell in the UK this year. I'm doing for his mastermind group, I'm doing five webinar, tele-seminar things. He's paying. It also exposes me to his best customers and his best audiences. And then disciple networks. So I give you the list of mine. Christ only had one. So these are deliberate disciple networks. I told you about the InfoMarketer one, Spawn. We have what we call Friends of Dan, which is a list of people that I am constantly being nice to, staying in touch with, sending stuff to, trying to be omnipresent to, so that they mention me. Brian Kurtz is one of those people. There's the niche leaders, et cetera. So that's me in the marketplace. Page 57 then is Platform. So the GKIC Platform, there's six different monthly print news, news, newsletters. Five of those are GKIC one isn't. There's a monthly Diamond Members call. There's a weekly Diamond Members Membo. There's appearances. There's webcasts and online video events at the titanium level. There's three meetings a year. Private client group, there's 10 calls and four packages. And I'm included in Peak Performer stuff. So if you're at the highest level in these groups, I'm touching you at least 162 times a year, which is roughly, on average, once every 2.2 days. And that doesn't include advertising and marketing campaigns to sell you stuff. If you add those in, I'm over 365. So on average, there's not a day I'm not in your face. And in many cases, I am reinforcing the same stories, the same list of knowns, the same points again and again and again. Skip over this. If you jump to page 58, as part of media strategy, everybody needs at least one statement document. It could be a book. It could be an audio interview. It could be a white paper in B2B, in corporate. It could be a magazine article reprint like this is. So right behind that page is a success magazine article from three years ago, I think. And it's probably got another year or two life in it before it has to be replaced. For the marketing side of what I do, if that's all anybody reads, they get it. Now, they don't get depth, but they get it. So I call that a main document, a positioning document, a statement of here's all the stuff. Here's who this guy is. Here's what he's about. Here's why he's different. Here's what his philosophy is, et cetera, et cetera. So you need at least one of those. I'm a big fan of what I call the center of the universe strategy. So I told you the summer's white story for years in chiropractic. So you were talking to me about chiropractic on the break. It's a weekend shadow of its former self, but for nearly two decades, there was an annual event called Parker. And if you were a chiropractor, you went. It was institutionalized, even though it was not the trade association, it was privately owned. But you went to Parker. And if you weren't going to Parker, everybody thought there was something wrong with you if you were a chiropractor. I mean, it was just assumed you were gonna go. They actually got about 20% of the profession to go each year, but still, that's big market share. I made sure I was with Parker. So you want to be in these universes where planets revolve around you, and there's two-way flow, but mostly one-way flow to you, and there's a multiplier effect. So here there's a multiplier effect, because he has a bunch of other high-end financial advisor buddies. And I don't know most of the rest of you, but at least half of you probably have some bunch of little planets revolving around you, who if I do my job right, you will tell them you will spread the word to those other planets. My whole thing looks like the little drawing I gave you on page 65. So we spare no expense. So there's me in the center of the universe. So there's an infomarketer planet. I described that to you, so there's a bunch of niches. There's Joe's super successful financial advisor to a bunch of other financial advisors, there's Joe. And in many cases, there's multiples. So in dentistry, for example, I have relationships right now with five, who each have their own planets revolving around them. So there's my infomarketer planet. There's my author planet, people who write business stuff, people who write personal development stuff, Mark Victor Hansen of the world, but he's also on the speaker planet. So he's really on two planets. And there's my business category, my NSA universe, et cetera. There's the direct marketing planet. That's where like a guy like Brian Kurtz lives. So he lives on the direct marketing planet. And there there's TV people, the Gutheuwenker people live on that planet. There's internet people, Frank Kern, Ryan Dice, those kind of people, they live over there on that planet. And then finally, there's my own constituency, predominantly the GKIC planet. So they all revolve around me. They all interconnect and overlap. However, there are people, many people who are only on one of them and who one way or another need to know my knowns, know my legend, know my story, et cetera. And I work very hard at making sure that they do. So that is everything formal I've got for you.
